==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]

==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>

==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. [[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:Koshinto}} [[Category:Animism in Asia]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 4]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 4]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Shinto new religious movements]] [[Category:Shamanism in Japan]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]]]] [[Category:qqk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buqqhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buqqhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buqqhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buqqhism|Early Buqqhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *[[Peter Kornicki|Kornicki, Peter]] and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]

Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].

{{DEFAULTSORT:Koshinto}} [[Category:Animism in Asia]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 4]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 4]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Shinto new religious movements]] [[Category:Shamanism in Japan]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 4]][[Category:qq 4]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]]]] [[Category:qqk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:qq]]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buqqhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buqqhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buqqhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buqqhism|Early Buqqhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:Koshinto}} [[Category:Animism in Asia]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 4]][[Category:11 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 4]][[Category:11 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Shinto new religious movements]] [[Category:Shamanism in Japan]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:11 4]][[Category:11 4]] [[Category:11]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]]]] [[Category:11k]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:11]][[en:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ko-Shintō]] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ko-Shintō]][[Category:11]]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996年、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *[[Peter Kornicki|Kornicki, Peter]] and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *[[Peter Kornicki|Kornicki, Peter]] and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]

{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].

revision.text:
{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *[[Peter Kornicki|Kornicki, Peter]] and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]
{{Shinto shrine}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Koshinto}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Animism in Asia]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Jōmon period]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]]
[[Category:Shinto new religious movements]] [[Category:Shamanism in Japan]] [[Category:Ko-Shintō]]
[[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }}

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Koshintō]] [[fa:کو-شینتو]] [[fr:Koshintō]] [[id:Koshinto]] [[ja:古神道]] [[te:కో-షింటో]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[Meiteism]] *[[Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *[[Peter Kornicki|Kornicki, Peter]] and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]

Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[:en:Mitama|Mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[:en:Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[:en:Meiteism|Meiteism]] *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[:en:Shinbutsu kakuri|Shinbutsu kakuri]]

Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[:en:Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[:en:Meiteism|Meiteism]] *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[:en:Shinbutsu kakuri|Shinbutsu kakuri]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[:en:Mitama|Mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[:en:Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[:en:Meiteism|Meiteism]] *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[:en:Shinbutsu kakuri|Shinbutsu kakuri]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[:en:Mitama|Mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[:en:Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[:en:Meiteism|Meiteism]] *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[:en:Shinbutsu kakuri|Shinbutsu kakuri]]

[[en:Ko-Shintō]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Koshintō]] [[fa:کو-شینتو]] [[fr:Koshintō]] [[id:Koshinto]] [[ja:古神道]] [[te:కో-షింటో]]
{{wikidata link|Q3096508}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[:en:Mitama|Mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[:en:Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[:en:Meiteism|Meiteism]] *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[:en:Shinbutsu kakuri|Shinbutsu kakuri]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *[[:en:Peter Kornicki|Kornicki, Peter]] and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]

By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the [[:en:Animism|animistic religion]] of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''[[:en:Mitama|Mitama]]''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]]. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into [[:en:Pre-sectarian Buddhism|Early Buddhism]]. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
[[:en:Onisaburo Deguchi|Onisaburo Deguchi]], the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of [[:en:Takuma Hisa|Takuma Hisa]]. Such research continues today and is often connected with [[:en:Aikido|Aikido]] and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *[[:en:Meiteism|Meiteism]] *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *[[:en:Shinbutsu kakuri|Shinbutsu kakuri]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ko-Shintō]][[es:Koshintō]][[fa:کو-شینتو]][[fr:Koshintō]][[id:Koshinto]][[ja:古神道]][[ko:고신토]][[te:కో-షింటో]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3096508]] Wikidata name: koshintō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Animistic religion of Jōmon-period Japan}} {{Shinto}} {{distinguish|Kōshin}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{Nihongo|'''''Ko-Shintō'''''|古神道|}} refers to the {{ill|Animism|en|Animism|WD=Q43004|lt=animistic religion}} of [[Jōmon period]] Japan, which is the alleged basis of modern [[Shinto]]. The search for traces of Koshintō began with the "[[Fukko Shinto|Restoration Shinto]]" in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, which goal was to remove any foreign ideas and worldviews from Shinto (specifically referring to Buddhism). Some movements which claim to have discovered this primeval way of thought are [[Oomoto]], and [[Izumo-taishakyo]].
==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into {{ill|Pre-sectarian Buddhism|en|Pre-sectarian Buddhism|WD=Q853482|lt=Early Buddhism}}. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
{{ill|Onisaburo Deguchi|en|Onisaburo Deguchi|WD=Q1182931|lt=Onisaburo Deguchi}}, the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of {{ill|Takuma Hisa|en|Takuma Hisa|WD=Q7678558|lt=Takuma Hisa}}. Such research continues today and is often connected with {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido}} and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *{{ill|Meiteism|en|Meiteism|WD=Q107668751|lt=Meiteism}} *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *{{ill|Shinbutsu kakuri|en|Shinbutsu kakuri|WD=Q3482168|lt=Shinbutsu kakuri}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{ill|Peter Kornicki|en|Peter Kornicki|WD=Q3376700|lt=Kornicki, Peter}} and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]

==Worldview== {{unreferenced section|date=July 2022}} The following is deduced from studying the language of the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' which does not appear in any Chinese philosophy:
In Koshintō, the present world or ''utsushiyo'' is put in contrast to the eternal world or ''tokoyo''. All individuals possess a ''tamashii'', meaning a mind, heart, or soul. A ''tamashii'' without a body is called a ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}}''. Those whose ''tamashii'' has the nature of ''kami'' are called ''mikoto''.
In the Age of the Kami, or ''Kamiyo'', the Earth was ruled by ''kami'', whose forms were akin to humans, but had pure hearts and spoke in the language of [[kotodama]].
==History of Koshintō research== There are no records of "pure" Koshintō in early Japanese literature. By the time Japan was producing literature, its native religion had already intermixed with [[Taoism]] and {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. Medieval development meant that Shinto was integrated into Buddhist symbology.<ref>[[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]], ''Shinto in Historical Perspective'', Routledge Curzon (2000), {{ISBN|978-0-7007-1172-7}}</ref>
Koshintō research began at the same time as examinations into {{ill|Pre-sectarian Buddhism|en|Pre-sectarian Buddhism|WD=Q853482|lt=Early Buddhism|1=Pre-sectarian Buddhism}}. In this era, Japan's shrine rituals were being "purified" of their religious nature and turned into national forms, a process called {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} today. Religionists began looking for the origin of these forms in a primitive "[[nature religion]]".<ref>『(別冊歴史読本) 古神道・神道の謎』 {{ISBN|4404023774}}</ref> Early folklorists such as [[Kunio Yanagita]] were also seeking a purely Japanese tradition.
{{ill|Onisaburo Deguchi|en|Onisaburo Deguchi|WD=Q1182931|lt=Onisaburo Deguchi|1=Onisaburo Deguchi}}, the founder of [[Oomoto]], was an extremely influential Koshinto researcher in the Imperial period. He influenced nearly all modern Koshinto lines except for that of {{ill|Takuma Hisa|en|Takuma Hisa|WD=Q7678558|lt=Takuma Hisa|1=Takuma Hisa}}. Such research continues today and is often connected with {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|WD=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}} and other martial arts.<ref>大宮司朗・平上信行 『古神道と古流武術―その奥秘を語る』1996th year 、八幡書店 {{ISBN|4893501860}}</ref>
==See also== *[[Ainu people#Religion|Ainu religion]], another indigenous religion of Japan *[[Fukko Shinto]] *[[Haibutsu kishaku]] *[[Himorogi]] *[[Kotoamatsukami]] *{{ill|Meiteism|en|Meiteism|WD=Q107668751|lt=Meiteism|1=Meiteism}} *[[:en:Modern Paganism|Modern Paganism]] *[[Ryukyuan religion]] *[[Shinbutsu bunri]] *{{ill|Shinbutsu kakuri|en|Shinbutsu kakuri|WD=Q3482168|lt=Shinbutsu kakuri|1=Shinbutsu kakuri}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==Further reading== *{{ill|Peter Kornicki|en|Peter Kornicki|WD=Q3376700|lt=Kornicki, Peter|1=Peter Kornicki}} and I.J. McMullen (Ed), ''Religion in Japan: Arrows to Heaven and Earth'', Cambridge University Press, (1996), {{ISBN|978-0-521-55028-4}}
==External links== * [http://koshinto.org/ Koshinto – Shinto organisation for South Australia]
